fields.rationale: Nix v. Whiteside concerns an attorney's Sixth Amendment duty not to present client perjury and does discuss the lawyer's dual obligations to client and to the integrity of the judicial process, but it is not a conflict-of-interest case and does not directly support the proposition's specific claim about the purpose of conflict-of-interest rules, making the fit thematic and weaker than represented.

The case is genuinely about excusable neglect under Rule 4(a)(5) and supports the general idea that misreading the rules is usually not excusable, but both quoted phrases in the proposition are fabricated—neither appears in the opinion, which actually applies Pioneer's equitable multi-factor test rather than stating a categorical 'strict' rule.

The Fifth Circuit affirmed denial of an opt-out extension on excusable-neglect (Pioneer factors) grounds because counsel received notice and could have inferred or inquired about the deadline, squarely supporting the proposition that delays within a party's control are not excusable neglect, with no quotes asserted.

The opinion shows Wilkens was represented by counsel of record (not pro se) and turns on Rule 4(a)(6) fax-notice of judgment, with no mention of the prison mailbox rule, directly contradicting the brief's characterization of 'unique circumstances' involving a pro se litigant and mailbox-rule confusion.

Celotex expressly describes summary judgment as integral to the Federal Rules, which are designed 'to secure the just, speedy and inexpensive determination of every action,' directly supporting the proposition.

The opinion directly holds that Rule 36 admissions, including those established by default for failure to respond, are conclusively established and cannot be rebutted by contrary testimony or ignored by the court, squarely supporting that deemed admissions are binding and cannot be contradicted.

The opinion supports that Rule 36 is self-executing and that default admissions are conclusively established, binding, and cannot be contradicted; the brief's summary-judgment framing is a fair application of this conclusive-effect holding even though the case itself arose at trial.

The opinion directly states Rule 36(a) admissions 'may be used for Rule 56 summary judgment,' supporting the substance, but the proposition is presented as a verbatim quote that does not appear in the opinion (FABRICATED), mandating downgrade to Yellow.

This Second Circuit opinion is misattributed to the Fifth Circuit, contradicts the proposition by emphasizing the court's discretion to permit withdrawal of admissions rather than that they 'may not be contradicted,' and the quoted phrase 'not mere evidence; they are conclusive judicial admissions' is fabricated and appears nowhere in the opinion.

In re Carney directly affirms a Fifth Circuit summary judgment based on Rule 36 deemed admissions and cites a line of cases doing the same, squarely supporting the proposition that the circuit consistently upholds such judgments.

The opinion supports the core deemed-admissions points (parts 1-3), but the quoted 'not mere evidence; they are conclusive judicial admissions' language does not appear (quote_check FABRICATED) and the prejudice-standard propositions (parts 4-5) about 'real impairment in obtaining evidence' are not articulated in this opinion.

The opinion expressly states the general rule that untimely discovery objections are waived ('when a party fails to object timely... objections thereto are waived'), which directly supports the cited proposition, even though the court carved a statutory-privilege exception on its specific facts.

Lacy v. Sitel directly supplies the three-factor good-cause test (willful default, prejudice, meritorious defense), the willfulness/intentional-failure standard, the 'mere delay does not alone constitute prejudice' rule with the loss-of-evidence/discovery/fraud-or-collusion exceptions, and the 'meritorious defense sufficient to support a finding on the merits' language, all matching the opinion closely (quote_check CLOSE, not fabricated).

The opinion addresses willful-versus-negligent default and is topically on point, but it does not support the brief's specific characterization that the failure to answer reflected a 'single negligent omission'—the primary defendant willfully defaulted, and relief for Ms. Carpanzano turned on lack of a meritorious defense and absent allegations, not an excusable single negligent omission, so the proposition oversimplifies the holding.

Rice v. HamiltonDavis establishes good cause under Rule 55(c) first (meritorious defense, lack of prejudice, default attributable to counsel) and only then imposes attorney's fees on defense counsel as a discretionary condition of vacatur, directly supporting the proposition that a fee award is a post-good-cause discretionary condition rather than a substitute for the threshold requirement.

McClain v. Lufkin directly holds that although the local forum prevailing rate is the presumptive key factor, it is not the sole determinant—out-of-district counsel's home rates may serve as the lodestar starting point in unusual circumstances, then adjusted by the Johnson factors—which accurately supports the proposition.

Kellstrom supports the general point that an attorney's customary billing rate is prima facie/presumptively reasonable, but the specific quoted sentence ('prima facie reasonable when he requests a rate within the range of rates he has charged paying clients... in the recent past') does not appear in the opinion, which instead ties prima facie reasonableness to the prevailing market/community rate.

Footnote 10 at page 288-289 of Missouri v. Jenkins states 'purely clerical or secretarial tasks should not be billed at a paralegal rate' while distinguishing such non-legal work from legitimate legal tasks, directly supporting the proposition that the secretarial-task caution does not extend to essential professional activities.

The opinion genuinely supports the underlying principle (a prevailing party is entitled to fees for time spent establishing and defending its fee claim, and denying such fees would 'dilute the fee award and be inconsistent with the purposes of § 1988'), but the specific quoted sentence presented in the brief does not appear verbatim in the opinion and quote_check is FABRICATED, requiring a downgrade to Yellow.

Anderson v. Liberty Lobby states verbatim that summary judgment will not lie if 'the evidence is such that a reasonable jury could return a verdict for the nonmoving party,' directly and accurately supporting the proposition.

Matsushita expressly states that on summary judgment the inferences drawn from the underlying facts must be viewed in the light most favorable to the party opposing the motion, directly and accurately supporting the cited proposition.

City of Madison v. Bryan was decided on standing, mootness, and Rule 11 sanction grounds and nowhere articulates the cited rule that a bill-of-exceptions appeal is limited to quasi-judicial/legislative (versus ministerial/administrative) board actions, so it does not support the proposition.

Stringer v. Trapp directly holds that whether a plaintiff should have discovered an injury with reasonable diligence often turns on the factual question of 'what the plaintiff knew and when,' which can be a jury question, and the court reversed summary judgment because those factual questions remained unanswered.

The case substantively supports that a breach-of-contract claim accrues at the time of the breach (Johnson v. Crisler quote), but the proposition's quoted wording is not verbatim from the opinion and the quote check is FABRICATED, requiring a downgrade to Yellow.

Franconia directly holds that a contract claim accrues at the time of breach (not on anticipation of a future breach) and that a repudiation ripens into a breach only when the promisee elects to treat it as such, precisely supporting both sub-propositions.

The opinion is a Sixth Circuit decision on Rule 56(d) discovery and summary judgment in an Equal Protection sex-discrimination case; it says nothing about surreplies or reply briefs raising new issues, so it does not support the cited proposition (and the brief miscites it as a 5th Cir. case).

North Cypress is an ERISA reimbursement/standing/preemption/RICO opinion that nowhere discusses surreplies or reply briefs raising new issues, and page 203 (the pin cite) addresses RICO distinctiveness, so the case does not support the proposition that the Fifth Circuit routinely permits surreplies.

The opinion holds that a First Amendment claim raised for the first time in response to a summary judgment motion 'is not properly before the court,' directly supporting the brief's characterization that Cutrera stands for the proposition that a plaintiff cannot raise a new cause of action at the summary judgment stage.

The 2025 Yilport opinion squarely reaffirms Mississippi's minutes rule—that a contract with a public board is formed only when its express terms are spread upon the board's minutes (and that such contracts 'may not be varied by parol nor altered by a court of equity'), directly and accurately supporting the cited proposition.

fields.brief_block: Deemed admissions are "judicial admissions" that are binding on the party and cannot be contradicted at trial or on a motion for summary judgment.
fields.opinion_block: In form and substance a Rule 36 admission is comparable to an admission in pleadings or a stipulation drafted by counsel for use at trial, rather than to an evidentiary admission of a party.... An admission that is not withdrawn or amended cannot be rebutted by contrary testimony or ignored by the district court simply because it finds the evidence presented by the party against whom the admission operates more credible.... This conclusive effect applies equally to those admissions made affirmatively and those established by default.
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion substantively supports the proposition: it holds that Rule 36 admissions are "conclusively established," that an admission "cannot be rebutted by contrary testimony or ignored by the district court," and that this conclusive effect "applies equally to those admissions made affirmatively and those established by default" -- i.e., deemed admissions bind the party and cannot be contradicted. The opinion's characterization that such an admission is "comparable to an admission in pleadings or a stipulation... rather than to an evidentiary admission of a party" is the substance behind labeling them "judicial admissions."

Two caveats keep this short of a clean quote match. The opinion never uses the literal phrase "judicial admissions" (hence the close/reworded quote), and because the case arose after a completed bench trial it does not itself mention summary judgment -- though the conclusive-effect language it states is general and not trial-specific, so the brief's reference to summary judgment is a routine application rather than a misstatement.

fields.brief_block: Rule 36 is self-executing: the City's failure to timely serve written answers results in conclusive judicial admissions. The Fifth Circuit recognizes that deemed admissions are judicial admissions that may not be contradicted and can alone support summary judgment. [...] Admissions are binding and cannot be contradicted to avoid summary judgment.
fields.opinion_block: Each matter on which an admission has been requested is admitted unless the party to whom the request is directed responds with a written answer or objection.... This conclusive effect applies equally to those admissions made affirmatively and those established by default, even if the matters admitted relate to material facts that defeat a party's claim.
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion supports the self-executing and binding aspects of the proposition: a matter is "admitted unless the party... responds with a written answer or objection," admissions are "conclusively established," and they "cannot be rebutted by contrary testimony or ignored by the district court" -- with this conclusive effect applying to admissions "established by default" even where they "relate to material facts that defeat a party's claim."

The brief overstates the case, however, when it invokes this authority for the propositions that deemed admissions "can alone support summary judgment" and "cannot be contradicted to avoid summary judgment." This case arose after a completed bench trial and addresses sua sponte post-trial withdrawal of admissions; it never discusses summary judgment or whether admissions alone suffice to grant it. Those summary-judgment-specific assertions extend the holding to a procedural posture the opinion did not decide, which is why the support is partial rather than full.

fields.brief_block: A genuine issue of material fact exists when the evidence is such that a reasonable jury could return a verdict for the non-moving party.
fields.opinion_block: More important for present purposes, summary judgment will not lie if the dispute about a material fact is "genuine," that is, if the evidence is such that a reasonable jury could return a verdict for the nonmoving party.
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion squarely supports this proposition: at page 248 the Court frames the "genuine" dispute standard as whether "the evidence is such that a reasonable jury could return a verdict for the nonmoving party," which is the canonical Anderson formulation the brief invokes.

The substance is accurate, but the sentence is presented as a verbatim quotation and is not one. The Court's actual phrasing is framed negatively — "summary judgment will not lie if the dispute about a material fact is 'genuine,' that is, if the evidence is such that a reasonable jury could return a verdict for the nonmoving party" — whereas the brief recasts the opening clause affirmatively as "A genuine issue of material fact exists when..." The operative tail tracks the opinion closely (the brief writes "non-moving" for the opinion's "nonmoving"), so the rewording is presentational rather than substantive.

fields.brief_block: The City's reliance on Cutrera v. Board of Supervisors of Louisiana State University, 429 F.3d 108 (5th Cir. 2005), is inapposite. That case stands for the unremarkable proposition that a plaintiff cannot raise an entirely new cause of action at the summary judgment stage.
fields.opinion_block: Appellant's First Amendment retaliation claim, however, was not raised in her complaint, and instead was raised for the first time in response to Appellee's motion for summary judgment. A claim which is not raised in the complaint but, rather, is raised only in response to a motion for summary judgment is not properly before the court.
fields.finding_analysis: Part C of Cutrera supports the brief's characterization: the court refused to consider the plaintiff's First Amendment retaliation claim under § 1983 because it "was not raised in her complaint, and instead was raised for the first time in response to Appellee's motion for summary judgment," holding that such a claim "is not properly before the court." The brief accurately distills this as the proposition that a plaintiff cannot raise a new cause of action at the summary judgment stage, and offers it only to characterize the case's limited reach rather than to extend its holding.

fields.brief_block: When determining whether to set aside default judgment, the Fifth Circuit directs courts to consider whether (1) the default was willful, (2) the non-defaulting party would be prejudiced, and (3) a meritorious defense has been presented. [...] willfulness turns on intentional failure to respond. [...] "mere delay does not alone constitute prejudice," but prejudice exists where delay results "in the loss of evidence, increased difficulties in discovery, or greater opportunities for fraud or collusion." In short, it is not a "defense sufficient to support a finding on the merits for the defaulting party."
fields.opinion_block: Thus, mere delay does not alone constitute prejudice. Rather, "the plaintiff must show that the delay will result in the loss of evidence, increased difficulties in discovery, or greater opportunities for fraud and collusion."
fields.finding_analysis:
Lacy v. Sitel is a Fifth Circuit decision on setting aside a default judgment for "good cause" under Rules 55(c) and 60(b), and it supports each sub-proposition. The opinion adopts the three-factor test verbatim ("whether the default was willful, whether setting it aside would prejudice the adversary, and whether a meritorious defense is presented"), frames willfulness as an "intentional failure of responsive pleadings," states that "mere delay does not alone constitute prejudice" with the loss-of-evidence/discovery/fraud formulation, and uses the exact phrase "meritorious defense sufficient to support a finding on the merits for the defaulting party."

The only discrepancy is a one-word quote substitution: the opinion reads "fraud and collusion," while the brief quotes "fraud or collusion." This is the basis for the CLOSE flag and does not affect the substantive support.

fields.brief_block: The Fifth Circuit has consistently upheld summary judgments based on deemed admissions.
fields.finding_analysis: In re Carney is itself a Fifth Circuit decision affirming summary judgment based on a Rule 36 deemed admission, and it notes that "this Court has affirmed a grant of summary judgment based on default admissions coupled with a district court's denial of a request to withdraw those admissions," citing Dukes; the opinion fairly grounds the brief's use of the case as a "See, e.g." example of the Fifth Circuit upholding such judgments.

fields.brief_block: (2) The Fifth Circuit recognizes that deemed admissions are judicial admissions that may not be contradicted and can alone support summary judgment. (3) Deemed admissions are "not mere evidence; they are conclusive judicial admissions" and are fully competent to support summary judgment. (4) The Fifth Circuit has explained that prejudice involves real impairment in obtaining evidence, not merely having to prove a case.
fields.opinion_block: Since Rule 36 admissions, whether express or by default, are conclusive as to the matters admitted, they cannot be overcome at the summary judgement stage by contradictory affidavit testimony or other evidence in the summary judgment record.
fields.finding_analysis: In re Carney directly supports the core propositions that a deemed Rule 36 admission conclusively establishes the validity of the underlying claim and can independently support summary judgment; the court held that Carney's failure to respond "conclusively established the validity" of the IRS's claim and that such admissions "cannot be overcome at the summary judgement stage by contradictory affidavit testimony." The brief overstates in two respects. First, the quoted phrase "not mere evidence; they are conclusive judicial admissions" does not appear in the opinion, which instead describes admissions as "conclusive as to the matters admitted" and never uses the term "judicial admissions." Second, the opinion does not articulate the prejudice principle the brief attributes to it — that prejudice "involves real impairment in obtaining evidence, not merely having to prove a case." The opinion's only prejudice language is that withdrawal must "not prejudice the party that obtained the admissions in its presentation of the case," without the elaboration the brief supplies.

fields.brief_block: Summary judgment is a procedural tool designed to secure the "just, speedy and inexpensive determination of every action.
fields.opinion_block: Summary judgment procedure is properly regarded not as a disfavored procedural shortcut, but rather as an integral part of the Federal Rules as a whole, which are designed "to secure the just, speedy and inexpensive determination of every action." Fed. Rule Civ. Proc. 1
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion at page 327 supports this proposition, characterizing summary judgment as an integral part of the Federal Rules, which are designed "to secure the just, speedy and inexpensive determination of every action," so the brief's framing of summary judgment as a procedural tool serving that purpose is accurate. The brief compresses the opinion slightly — the opinion attributes that design to the Federal Rules "as a whole" and places summary judgment within them, rather than saying summary judgment alone was designed for that end — but this is a fair and substantively accurate characterization, and the quoted phrase matches the opinion verbatim.

fields.brief_block: Mississippi courts have long held that a bill of exceptions statutory appeal is limited to challenges of a board's quasi-judicial or legislative actions, not its ministerial or administrative functions.
fields.opinion_block: Each instance was clearly a separate legislative act by the City. ... [E]ach bill of exception is a separate distinct legislative act of the City.
fields.finding_analysis:
City of Madison v. Bryan is a standing-and-mootness decision arising from a bill of exceptions appeal of a municipal permit denial. It does not address the scope limitation the brief asserts. The majority resolves the appeal by holding that Bryan lacked standing because he had no valid option or interest in the property when he filed his bill of exceptions, that the controversy was therefore moot, and that the circuit court's Rule 11 attorney's-fee award must be reversed for lack of sufficient findings and authority.

The opinion does use the vocabulary of the proposition in passing, characterizing the City's actions and "each bill of exception" as a "legislative act of the City." But it uses that phrase descriptively, not as a rule defining what may be appealed. Nowhere does the opinion hold — or even discuss — that a bill of exceptions statutory appeal is confined to quasi-judicial or legislative actions and excludes ministerial or administrative functions. That quasi-judicial/legislative versus ministerial/administrative distinction, which is the core of the brief's proposition, does not appear in the case, so the citation attributes a principle the opinion never adopted.

fields.brief_block: Admissions obtained under Rule 36, including those deemed admitted through a party's failure to respond, can form the basis for summary judgment.
fields.opinion_block: Carls has plainly made Rule 36(a) admissions. These may be used for Rule 56 summary judgment.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports the proposition: Carls' Rule 36(a) admissions arose precisely because it "failed to respond to the request," and the court holds those admissions "may be used for Rule 56 summary judgment," affirming the grant of summary judgment on that basis. The substance is squarely on point. The defect is presentational only — the entire sentence is offered as a verbatim quotation (and flagged FABRICATED), but no such sentence appears in the opinion; it is the brief's own paraphrase of the court's much shorter holding.

fields.brief_block: The admissions establish every element for judgment. The Fifth Circuit recognizes that deemed admissions are judicial admissions that may not be contradicted and can alone support summary judgment. Deemed admissions are "not mere evidence; they are conclusive judicial admissions" and are fully competent to support summary judgment.
fields.opinion_block: Under Rule 36(b), the decision to excuse the defendant from its admissions ... is in the court's discretion.
fields.finding_analysis:
Carls Drug is a Second Circuit FLSA decision, not a Fifth Circuit case, and it does not articulate the principle the brief attributes to "the Fifth Circuit" — that deemed admissions are "conclusive judicial admissions" that "may not be contradicted." The Fifth Circuit authorities the opinion does cite (Usery, Brennan, Coleman) go to the professional-employee exemption and willfulness, not to the conclusive character of admissions. The opinion supports only the narrower points that Rule 36 admissions can support summary judgment and were sufficient to establish the elements here ("It should be sufficient that Carls has admitted that it is an employer subject to FLSA and has failed to pay its pharmacists the overtime pay clearly due to them").

The brief overstates by casting admissions as uncontradictable. The opinion holds the opposite emphasis: under Rule 36(b) "the decision to excuse the defendant from its admissions ... is in the court's discretion," and the court "may permit withdrawal" in appropriate circumstances — undercutting the "may not be contradicted" framing. The quoted phrase "not mere evidence; they are conclusive judicial admissions" does not appear in the opinion.

fields.brief_block: (1) The right to sue accrues at the moment of breach, not on the mere suspicion that a breach might occur. (2) Franconia Assocs. v. U.S. holds that a claim for repudiation of a contract accrues at the time the repudiation is treated as a breach.
fields.opinion_block: Such a repudiation ripens into a breach prior to the time for performance only if the promisee "elects to treat it as such." ... "[t]he time of accrual ... depends on whether the injured party chooses to treat the ... repudiation as a present breach."
fields.finding_analysis: The cited page (143) and the surrounding accrual discussion squarely support both propositions: the opinion holds that the FmHA loan contracts were not presently breached by ELIHPA's enactment, that "[t]he cause of action for breach of contract accrues, and the statute of limitations begins to run, at the time of the breach," and that a mere repudiation (an announced refusal to perform in the future) "ripens into a breach prior to the time for performance only if the promisee 'elects to treat it as such,'" with accrual accelerated to the date of that election. The brief's gloss that accrual does not turn on "mere suspicion that a breach might occur" fairly captures the Court's holding that anticipatory repudiation alone does not start the clock, and part (2)'s statement that a repudiation claim accrues when the repudiation is treated as a breach mirrors the opinion's election rule.

fields.brief_block: It would be inconsistent with the purpose of the fee-shifting statute to dilute a fees award by refusing to compensate the attorney for the time reasonably spent in establishing and negotiating his rightful claim to the fee.
fields.opinion_block: It is settled that a prevailing plaintiff is entitled to attorney's fees for the effort entailed in litigating a fee claim and securing compensation. ... The time spent so replying is compensable under § 1988. To hold otherwise would dilute the fee award and be inconsistent with the purposes of § 1988.
fields.finding_analysis:
Cruz v. Hauck supports the proposition's substance. At page 1234 the court holds that a prevailing plaintiff is entitled to fees for time spent litigating and establishing a fee claim, and that to deny such compensation "would dilute the fee award and be inconsistent with the purposes of § 1988" — which is precisely the principle the brief attributes to the case.

The quotation marks are the only problem. The brief presents a polished sentence as a verbatim quote, but those exact words do not appear in the opinion; the court's actual language is shorter and speaks of "litigating" rather than "negotiating" a fee claim. The principle is accurately captured, so the proposition is supported on substance even though the quote itself is fabricated.

fields.brief_block: Defendant's failure to respond constitutes a waiver of any objections it might have asserted to Plaintiff's discovery requests.
fields.opinion_block: We readily agree with the district court that as a general rule, when a party fails to object timely to interrogatories, production requests, or other discovery efforts, objections thereto are waived. But that general rule is not applicable here.
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion does state the general waiver rule the brief invokes, and the pinpoint cite to page 1156 is accurate: the Fifth Circuit "readily agree[s]" that when a party fails to object timely to discovery, its objections are waived. To that extent the proposition is grounded in the text.

The overstatement is that this case is, on its facts, an exception to the rule rather than an application of it. The court immediately adds "that general rule is not applicable here," holds that the government's untimely response did not waive its objection to medical quality assurance records protected by 10 U.S.C. § 1102, and grants mandamus vacating the very order that had found waiver and compelled production. So the brief cites for a clean waiver result a case whose holding refused to enforce waiver. The general-rule statement is supportive dicta, but the decided outcome runs the other way, which a citing brief should flag rather than present as straightforward waiver authority.

fields.brief_block: Courts in the Fifth Circuit routinely permit surreplies where a reply brief raises new issues to which the opposing party had no prior opportunity to respond.
fields.finding_analysis:
Doe v. City of Memphis is a Sixth Circuit case about whether the district court abused its discretion by granting summary judgment and striking class allegations before the plaintiff had a full opportunity to conduct discovery under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 56(d). It does not address surreplies, reply briefs raising new issues, or the opposing party's opportunity to respond to a reply.

The opinion is authored by Circuit Judge Clay of the Sixth Circuit and turns entirely on the Plott/Rule 56(d) discovery factors and Rule 23 class certification — not on surreply practice and not on Fifth Circuit law. The word "surreply" appears nowhere in the opinion. The proposition and the cited court (5th Cir.) are both foreign to this case, so the citation provides no support for the brief's assertion.

fields.brief_block: An attorney's customary billing rate is presumptively reasonable. 'An attorney's requested hourly rate is prima facie reasonable when he requests a rate that is within the range of rates he has charged paying clients for similar work in the recent past.'
fields.opinion_block: When an attorney's customary billing rate is the rate at which the attorney requests the lodestar be computed and that rate is within the range of prevailing market rates, the court should consider this rate when fixing the hourly rate to be allowed. When that rate is not contested, it is prima facie reasonable. ... see also Powell, 891 F.2d at 1175 (holding customary billing rate to be prima facie reasonable).
fields.finding_analysis:
The core proposition is supported. At page 328 the opinion holds that an attorney's customary billing rate is prima facie reasonable, quoting Islamic Center ("When that rate is not contested, it is prima facie reasonable") and citing Powell as "holding customary billing rate to be prima facie reasonable."

The sentence the brief sets off in single quotation marks, however, does not appear in Kellstrom. The opinion anchors prima facie reasonableness to a customary rate that is "within the range of prevailing market rates" and is "not contested" — not to a rate "within the range of rates he has charged paying clients for similar work in the recent past." The substance of the proposition tracks the holding, but the quoted formulation is a recast paraphrase presented as a verbatim quote rather than Kellstrom's actual language.

fields.brief_block: The Fifth Circuit has consistently held that "the excusable neglect standard is a strict one" and that "inadvertence, ignorance of the rules, or mistakes construing the rules do not usually constitute excusable neglect."
fields.opinion_block: Where, as here, the rule at issue is unambiguous, a district court's determination that the neglect was inexcusable is virtually unassailable. [...] The nature of this very mistake "weighs heavily against a finding of excusable neglect."
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion substantively supports the proposition: it holds that counsel's misreading of an unambiguous procedural rule (Rule 6(e)) does not constitute excusable neglect under Appellate Rule 4(a)(5), describing such a determination as "virtually unassailable" and the mistake as weighing "heavily against a finding of excusable neglect," while leaving open only the "rare case" in which a rule misinterpretation might qualify. The core principle the brief draws — that the standard is applied strictly and that mistakes construing the rules usually do not amount to excusable neglect — tracks the opinion's reasoning and its reversal of the magistrate's extension.

Neither quoted string, however, appears anywhere in the opinion. The language "inadvertence, ignorance of the rules, or mistakes construing the rules do not usually constitute excusable neglect" is the Supreme Court's formulation from Pioneer, and this opinion quotes only Pioneer's equitable-factors passage, not that line; the phrase "the excusable neglect standard is a strict one" likewise does not appear. The substance is sound, but the brief presents fabricated quotations as though they were drawn from this case.

fields.brief_block: The court must view all evidence and draw all reasonable inferences in the light most favorable to the nonmoving party.
fields.opinion_block: Respondents correctly note that "[o]n summary judgment the inferences to be drawn from the underlying facts . . . must be viewed in the light most favorable to the party opposing the motion."
fields.finding_analysis: At the pinpoint page (475 U.S. at 587-588), the Matsushita opinion endorses this summary-judgment standard, stating that respondents "correctly note" that inferences drawn from the underlying facts must be viewed in the light most favorable to the party opposing the motion (quoting United States v. Diebold, Inc., 369 U.S. 654, 655 (1962)). The proposition is directly and accurately supported. The only nuance — not enough to downgrade this general procedural statement — is that the very next sentence cautions that "antitrust law limits the range of permissible inferences from ambiguous evidence in a § 1 case," a qualification specific to the antitrust merits rather than the baseline standard the brief invokes.

fields.brief_block: While the prevailing rate in the district where the court sits is a key factor, it is not the only one in determining how much to award in attorney's fees.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion supports this proposition. At page 381 and following, McClain treats the local forum prevailing rate as the presumptive starting point for the lodestar ("reasonable" rates "are to be calculated according to the prevailing market rates in the relevant community"; local rates "set[] a floor for compensation"), but it makes equally clear that this rate is not the sole determinant: the court applies "the twelve Johnson adjustment factors" to the lodestar, "retains discretion to adjust the lodestar and achieve an overall reasonable fee award," and in unusual cases where out-of-district counsel are proven necessary uses those counsel's home rates as the starting point instead. The proposition that the local rate is a key factor but not the only one in setting fees is thus directly and accurately grounded in the opinion.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court's caution against billing for purely secretarial tasks in Missouri v. Jenkins, 491 U.S. 274, 288 n.10 (1989), does not extend to essential professional activities, such as emailing or calling a client or reviewing and drafting legal documents.
fields.opinion_block: Of course, purely clerical or secretarial tasks should not be billed at a paralegal rate, regardless of who performs them. What the court in Johnson v. Georgia Highway Express, Inc. . . . said in regard to the work of attorneys is applicable by analogy to paralegals: "It is appropriate to distinguish between legal work, in the strict sense, and investigation, clerical work, compilation of facts and statistics and other work which can often be accomplished by non-lawyers . . . . Such non-legal work may command a lesser rate."
fields.finding_analysis:
The cited pinpoint (491 U.S. at 288, footnote 10) is exactly where this distinction lives: the Court cautions that "purely clerical or secretarial tasks should not be billed at a paralegal rate," while expressly contrasting that with compensable "legal work, in the strict sense" and substantive paralegal tasks the same footnote lists (e.g., factual investigation, document production, checking citations, and "drafting correspondence"). The brief's proposition — that the secretarial-task caution does not reach essential professional/legal activities — faithfully captures the footnote's line between non-compensable clerical work and compensable professional work.

The brief's specific illustrations (emailing or calling a client, reviewing and drafting legal documents) are not all named verbatim in the opinion, but they are reasonable instances of the "legal work" side of the distinction the footnote draws, so the gloss does not overstate the holding.

fields.brief_block: Courts have consistently recognized that the purpose of the rules governing conflicts of interest is not only to protect the interests of clients but also to maintain the integrity of the judicial process.
fields.opinion_block: an attorney's ethical duty to advance the interests of his client is limited by an equally solemn duty to comply with the law and standards of professional conduct; it specifically ensures that the client may not use false evidence.
fields.finding_analysis:
Nix v. Whiteside is a Sixth Amendment ineffective-assistance case about whether counsel violates the right to counsel by refusing to help a client present perjured testimony; its subject is the attorney's duty of candor to the tribunal, not the rules governing conflicts of interest. The opinion genuinely supports the second half of the proposition — that an attorney's obligations run not only to the client but also to the integrity of the judicial process — describing the lawyer as "an officer of the court and a key component of a system of justice, dedicated to a search for truth" and holding that the duty to advance client interests is "limited by an equally solemn duty to comply with the law and standards of professional conduct."

But the Court expressly distinguished the client-perjury "conflict" before it from a true conflict of interest, stating that "this is not remotely the kind of conflict of interests dealt with in Cuyler v. Sullivan." The brief thus recharacterizes a candor/perjury holding as a statement about the purpose of conflict-of-interest rules, a principle Nix does not articulate. The pinpoint at page 166 falls in the passage where the Court declines to define the relative weight of ethics canons and the Sixth Amendment because "virtually all of the sources speak with one voice" — language that does not state the cited proposition.

fields.brief_block: Courts in the Fifth Circuit routinely permit surreplies where a reply brief raises new issues to which the opposing party had no prior opportunity to respond.
fields.finding_analysis: North Cypress Medical Center Operating Co. v. Cigna Healthcare is a Fifth Circuit ERISA/insurance-reimbursement case about whether a hospital had standing to enforce assigned plan benefits, ERISA preemption of Texas prompt-payment provisions, the sufficiency of RICO pleadings, an unsealing order, and the limitations period for compulsory counterclaims. It does not address surreplies, reply briefs raising new issues, or a party's opportunity to respond. The pinpoint cite to page 203 falls within the RICO § 1962(c) discussion of the person/enterprise distinctiveness requirement, which has nothing to do with the briefing-procedure proposition the brief attributes to the case. There is no language anywhere in the opinion supporting a rule about permitting surreplies.

fields.brief_block: Nor can a fee award substitute for Rule 55(c)'s threshold requirements; it is a discretionary condition available once "good cause" is established, not a device to manufacture it.
fields.opinion_block: Considering the lack of prejudice to plaintiff, along with the fact that the event of default ... was attributable to counsel ... and further considering that HamiltonDavis has presented meritorious defenses, the court concludes that the default judgment as to HamiltonDavis should be set aside. However, the court finds that plaintiff's reasonable attorney's fees and costs ... should be borne by defense counsel.
fields.finding_analysis:
Rice applies the Rule 55(c) "good cause" standard and analyzes the three set-aside factors (willfulness, meritorious defense, prejudice) entirely on their own terms before turning to fees. Only after concluding that good cause existed to set aside the default judgment as to HamiltonDavis does the court order that plaintiff's attorney's fees and costs be borne by defense counsel. To that extent the opinion is consistent with the brief's second clause -- the fee award is imposed as a discretionary consequence after good cause is established, not as part of the threshold analysis.

But the opinion never articulates the brief's affirmative principle that a fee award "cannot substitute for Rule 55(c)'s threshold requirements" or is "not a device to manufacture" good cause. The court was never presented with, and did not address, any argument that fees could be used to satisfy or generate the good-cause showing. The brief draws a normative rule from the sequencing of the court's ruling that the court itself did not state, so the case supports the structure of the proposition while overstating it as a holding on what a fee award cannot do.

fields.brief_block: Scott v. Carpanzano is inopposite because there, the failure to answer reflected a single negligent omission.
fields.opinion_block: While their reliance on Mr. Carpanzano acting with the attorneys he retained may have been negligent, it does not amount to an “ ‘intentional failure’ to respond to litigation.”
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion does touch on negligence: as to Ms. Belcastro and Ms. Carpanzano, the court found their reliance on Mr. Carpanzano "may have been negligent" but did not rise to an intentional failure to respond, so to that limited extent the brief is engaging with the actual subject matter. But the characterization that the failure to answer "reflected a single negligent omission" oversimplifies the holding. The opinion describes ongoing reliance on a family member across the proceedings, not a discrete one-time omission, and it never frames the case around "a single negligent omission." More importantly, the controlling defendant, Mr. Carpanzano, was found to have *willfully* defaulted through repeated, intentional conduct (instructing counsel not to appear, ceasing communication), the opposite of negligence, and his default judgment was affirmed on that basis. Reducing this multi-defendant, mixed willful/negligent analysis to "a single negligent omission" overstates and flattens what the court actually decided.

fields.brief_block: The Fifth Circuit has consistently held that delays within a party's control do not constitute excusable neglect.
fields.opinion_block: The district court did not abuse its discretion in refusing to extend the opt-out date, because it considered Silvercreek's proffered evidence and determined that counsel's performance fell below the threshold required for neglect to be excusable.
fields.finding_analysis:
Silvercreek is directly on point as to excusable neglect: applying the Pioneer factors, the Fifth Circuit affirmed the district court's refusal to extend an opt-out deadline because the delay was attributable to counsel's own failure to track a deadline it had notice of — a delay squarely within the party's control. That substance lends real support to the brief's proposition.

The brief nonetheless overstates the holding. This is a single decision reviewed for abuse of discretion and resting on case-specific facts (counsel received the order, should have noticed the blank opt-out date, and could have inquired), not a categorical rule that the court has "consistently held." Indeed, the opinion cuts against treating excusable neglect as mechanical: it notes that "Silvercreek incorrectly contends that the district court must rigorously apply each of these factors in every case," emphasizing a discretionary, multi-factor analysis. Recasting that fact-bound, deferential ruling as a consistently-held categorical principle extends the case beyond what it decided.

fields.brief_block: Under Mississippi law, the question of when a plaintiff should have discovered an injury through the exercise of reasonable diligence is an issue of fact to be decided by a jury.
fields.opinion_block: [O]ccasionally the question of whether the suit is barred by the statute of limitations is a question of fact for the jury; however, as with other putative fact questions, the question may be taken away from the jury if reasonable minds could not differ as to the conclusion.
fields.finding_analysis:
Stringer does address whether the limitations/discovery question is a jury issue: it observes that the discovery-rule inquiry "often turns on the factual determination of 'what the plaintiff knew and when,'" and that the limitations question can be "a question of fact for the jury." But the opinion frames this as a conditional, not the categorical rule the brief asserts. The court says the question is "[o]ccasionally" one for the jury, and expressly adds that "the question may be taken away from the jury if reasonable minds could not differ as to the conclusion" — which is exactly how summary judgment can still be entered on limitations grounds.

The brief drops those qualifiers and presents the proposition as an unconditional rule that the reasonable-diligence/discovery question "is an issue of fact to be decided by a jury." That overstates the holding; the opinion supports the topic but expressly preserves the court's power to resolve the issue as a matter of law when reasonable minds could not differ, making this partial rather than full support.

fields.brief_block: Yilport reaffirmed the long-standing rule that a contract with a public board is formed only when entered on the minutes and that its terms cannot be varied by parol evidence.
fields.opinion_block: Such contracts when so entered upon the minutes may not be varied by parol nor altered by a court of equity[.]
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion squarely supports both halves of the proposition. On the minutes-rule formation point, the Court "recently reaffirmed its long-standing holding that 'public boards speak only through their minutes,'" and held that "where a public board engages in business with another entity, no contract can be implied or presumed, it must be stated in express terms and recorded on the official minutes" (¶¶24-25). On the parol-evidence point, the Court quoted Butler that "[s]uch contracts when so entered upon the minutes may not be varied by parol nor altered by a court of equity" (¶25). The brief accurately characterizes the holding it reaffirms; no overstatement.

fields.brief_block: Wilkens involved unique circumstances not present here, including a pro se litigant and confusion regarding the application of the prison mailbox rule.
fields.opinion_block: counsel for Wilkens avers that he never received a copy until, at the request of the staff attorney for the district court, a copy was "faxed" to him by the court on September 7, 1999. ... Then as now, counsel of record for Wilkens was Mark W. Breding, Esq., Tyler, Texas.
fields.finding_analysis:
The brief attributes two specific circumstances to Wilkens that the opinion does not support. First, Wilkens was not a pro se litigant: the opinion refers throughout to "counsel for Wilkens," "Wilkens's lawyer," and "counsel of record," and a footnote names his attorney as Mark W. Breding, Esq. The pro se characterization is affirmatively contradicted by the text.

Second, the opinion has nothing to do with the prison mailbox rule. The case turns entirely on Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 4(a) — specifically whether a faxed copy of the judgment received in hand by counsel triggered subpart (A)'s seven-day window to file a Rule 4(a)(6) motion to reopen the time for a notice of appeal. The court never discusses, applies, or expresses confusion about the prison mailbox rule (the doctrine deeming a pro se prisoner's filing complete on delivery to prison officials). Because both of the asserted "unique circumstances" are absent from — and in the case of pro se status, contradicted by — the opinion, the proposition is unsupported.

fields.brief_block: The statute of limitations for a breach of contract claim begins to run at the time of the breach.
fields.opinion_block: [i]n the case of a breach of contract, the cause of action accrues at the time of the breach... .
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion substantively supports the proposition. Resolving the insurer's limitations defense, the Court explains that its accrual-at-breach ruling "is consistent with prior decisions of this Court where we held that '[i]n the case of a breach of contract, the cause of action accrues at the time of the breach... .'" (quoting Johnson v. Crisler). The point at which the cause of action accrues is the point at which the limitations period begins to run, so the brief's proposition is accurate.

The brief's quoted sentence does not appear verbatim in the opinion; it is a reworded restatement of the opinion's parallel language, substituting "statute of limitations ... begins to run" for the Court's "cause of action accrues." The substance is sound, but the quotation marks overstate the fidelity to the opinion's actual text.
